definition: A title for the Buddha that is usually best left untranslated in source-facing work.
notes: The project leaves Sugata untranslated by default because it functions as a title and English renderings tend to sound forced or overinterpretive. A short gloss is acceptable when the line needs help on first occurrence. The epithet belongs inside the cluster of Buddha-titles and should be treated as honorific and doctrinally loaded rather than decorative. The OSF glossary likewise keeps Sugata untranslated as a Buddha-title while allowing only a light gloss when needed.

context_rules.context: In most source-facing and doctrinal contexts
context_rules.notes: Default project practice is to leave the title untranslated.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose where a gloss is necessary
context_rules.notes: Use sparingly and only as a gloss.

context_rules.context: When the passage is explicitly using a Buddha-epithet in praise or recollection
context_rules.notes: Preserve the title as a title rather than paraphrasing it away.

example_phrases.notes: Common titular usage in praise formulas.

authority_basis.scope: Named in notes as support for the current Sugata policy.

translation_policy.default_scope: Use 'Sugata' as the default rendering in the entry's governed translation contexts unless a recorded context rule overrides it.
translation_policy.when_not_to_apply: Do not force an English substitute when the local passage needs the Pali term; use a gloss only when the entry or passage explicitly calls for one.
translation_policy.drift_risk: Prevents drift back toward 'saint' or other unrecorded alternates.
translation_policy.leave_untranslated_when: Leave the term untranslated when source-facing doctrinal range or local explanation would be narrowed too quickly by an English equivalent.
